queries.persona: 3 years fullstack development experience (C#/.NET, React). Currently in clinical research software. Goal: move to a fintech company in New York City.
queries.query: I'm looking for projects to boost my chances at fintech companies when moving to new york city. I have 3 years fullstack development experience

plan.interpretation: You want to identify cutting-edge research projects in quantitative finance, algorithmic trading, risk modeling, and financial data analysis that you can implement as portfolio demonstrations to strengthen your candidacy at fintech firms.
plan.anchorText: algorithmic trading, portfolio optimization, market microstructure, high-frequency trading, option pricing, risk management, financial time series, machine learning trading, volatility forecasting, derivatives pricing, quantitative finance, backtesting, statistical arbitrage, market prediction, deep learning finance, reinforcement learning trading, anomaly detection markets, financial data analysis
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a scalable end-to-end system for real-time financial data ingestion, processing, and algorithmic decision-making. Our architecture combines distributed computing with machine learning pipelines to predict short-term price movements and optimize portfolio rebalancing decisions. We validate the framework on historical market data across multiple asset classes, demonstrating 15-30% improvement in risk-adjusted returns compared to baseline strategies. The system achieves sub-millisecond latency for order execution and handles datasets at petabyte scale. Code and benchmarks are available for reproducibility.

queries.persona: Backend engineer, 4 years, mostly payments infrastructure in Java. Wants to specialize in fraud and risk.
queries.query: recent machine learning approaches to payment fraud detection that I could implement as a demo system

plan.interpretation: You're looking for recent machine learning papers on payment fraud detection that are implementable as a demo project.
plan.anchorText: payment fraud detection, transaction fraud, machine learning, neural networks, anomaly detection, classification, real-time fraud detection, financial transaction analysis, imbalanced data, feature engineering, graph neural networks, deep learning, fraud risk scoring, behavioral modeling
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We propose a machine learning approach for detecting fraudulent payment transactions in real-time financial systems. Our method combines supervised classification with anomaly detection to identify fraudulent activity while minimizing false positives on legitimate transactions. We evaluate our approach on a large-scale payment dataset, achieving 0.94 AUC-ROC on held-out test transactions. We demonstrate implementation considerations including handling severe class imbalance, latency constraints, and feature engineering from transaction metadata. Our results show significant improvements over baseline fraud detection systems and provide practical guidance for production deployment.

queries.persona: Software engineer with solid Python, no finance background. Curious about algorithmic trading.
queries.query: reinforcement learning for trading strategies - something small enough to reproduce with free market data

plan.interpretation: You want to find papers on reinforcement learning applied to trading strategy development, focused on approaches reproducible with freely available market data and realistic for a solo implementation project.
plan.anchorText: reinforcement learning trading strategy, RL agent portfolio optimization, deep Q-learning market prediction, policy gradient trading algorithms, reward function asset trading, free market data backtesting, stock price prediction RL, transaction cost constraints trading, financial time series forecasting, DQN trader, actor-critic trading, state representation financial markets, exploratory trading agents
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a reinforcement learning framework for automated intraday trading strategy development. The approach employs a deep Q-network agent trained on open-source market data to learn optimal buy-sell decisions from raw OHLCV candlestick sequences. The agent is trained using experience replay and target networks on publicly available historical price feeds without requiring proprietary data. Backtesting on held-out periods demonstrates consistent Sharpe ratio improvements over baseline strategies with modest computational requirements suitable for commodity hardware. We provide open-source code and detailed reproduction instructions using standard Python libraries.

queries.persona: Quant-curious developer, strong C++, wants to understand how markets work at the order level.
queries.query: limit order book modeling and market microstructure research

plan.interpretation: You're looking for papers on limit order book dynamics, market microstructure theory, and order-level market mechanisms that could ground a quantitative trading or market simulation project.
plan.anchorText: limit order book, market microstructure, order flow, price formation, bid-ask spread, high-frequency trading, market impact, order dynamics, agent-based models, market simulation, order matching, liquidity provision, transaction costs, market efficiency, statistical arbitrage
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a stochastic model of limit order book dynamics incorporating realistic order arrival and cancellation patterns. The model captures the joint evolution of bid-ask spreads, order queue positions, and price impact through coupled differential equations with empirical parameters extracted from high-frequency market data. We demonstrate that the model accurately reproduces stylized facts of financial microstructure including mean-reverting spreads, non-linear price impact, and the heavy-tailed distribution of order lifetimes. Simulation experiments validate the framework for studying execution algorithms and measuring market resilience under various order flow regimes. The approach enables efficient pricing of latency-sensitive strategies and prediction of short-term price movements from order flow imbalance.

queries.persona: Data analyst moving toward quant finance, comfortable with pandas and basic optimization.
queries.query: modern portfolio optimization methods beyond mean-variance, ideally with code or reproducible experiments

plan.interpretation: You want to discover modern portfolio optimization techniques that go beyond classical mean-variance models, with preference for papers that include reproducible code or experimental implementations.
plan.anchorText: portfolio optimization, mean-variance analysis, risk parity, convex optimization, multi-objective optimization, robust optimization, machine learning portfolio construction, deep reinforcement learning trading, factor models, covariance estimation, shrinkage estimators, constraint portfolios, backtesting, asset allocation, portfolio rebalancing, Markowitz, efficient frontier
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We propose a robust portfolio optimization framework that extends classical mean-variance analysis by incorporating higher-order moments and tail risk constraints through a multi-objective optimization formulation. Using convex optimization techniques, we simultaneously optimize Sharpe ratio and maximum drawdown across a universe of liquid assets while enforcing diversification constraints. We provide a fully reproducible implementation in Python with backtesting on fifteen years of historical market data, demonstrating significant out-of-sample performance improvements and reduced drawdowns compared to equal-weight and traditional mean-variance benchmarks across multiple market regimes.

queries.persona: ML engineer at a bank, needs models that regulators can audit.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Credit risk assessment and loan default prediction require models that satisfy regulatory scrutiny while maintaining predictive accuracy. We propose a framework combining gradient boosting with inherently interpretable feature engineering and SHAP-based local explanations to quantify individual feature contributions to default predictions. Our approach is validated on large-scale credit datasets, demonstrating comparable performance to black-box alternatives while providing audit trails suitable for regulatory examination. We show that interpretability constraints need not substantially compromise predictive power in loan default classification, and we provide post-hoc explanation methods for existing production models that enable compliance auditing.

queries.persona: Fullstack developer, 3 years, wants a substantial ML portfolio piece.

plan.interpretation: You want to build a portfolio project comparing transformer-based and classical machine learning approaches for financial time series forecasting, using recent state-of-the-art methods.
plan.anchorText: time series forecasting, transformer models, attention mechanisms, LSTM, autoregressive models, financial data, stock price prediction, volatility forecasting, sequence-to-sequence, deep learning benchmarks, classical statistical methods, ARIMA, neural network architectures, temporal dependencies, multivariate forecasting
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: This paper presents a comprehensive empirical study comparing transformer-based architectures with classical time series forecasting methods on financial datasets. We evaluate self-attention mechanisms, multi-head attention, and positional encodings against ARIMA, exponential smoothing, and LSTM baselines across multiple financial instruments including stock indices and exchange rates. Our transformer model incorporates temporal feature embeddings and achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks, with detailed ablation studies isolating the contribution of attention components. We provide extensive comparisons on metric dimensions including directional accuracy, mean absolute percentage error, and Sharpe ratio for trading signals, offering practitioners guidance on method selection and computational trade-offs.

queries.persona: Web developer interested in blockchain engineering roles.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a comprehensive analysis of decentralized exchange (DEX) mechanics on Ethereum, examining how automated market maker (AMM) designs affect price discovery, slippage, and user outcomes. Using on-chain transaction data spanning six months, we characterize liquidity provision patterns, measure maximal extractable value (MEV) across protocol variants, and evaluate incentive structures that drive capital formation. Our findings reveal substantial inefficiencies in current pool designs and propose modifications to smart contract mechanics that improve execution quality for traders while maintaining incentive compatibility for liquidity providers. Code and datasets are released for reproducibility.

queries.persona: Actuarial background, learning to code, targeting risk roles at trading firms.

plan.interpretation: You're looking for papers on value at risk (VaR), tail risk estimation, and contemporary financial risk modeling techniques applicable to trading and risk management.
plan.anchorText: value at risk, VaR, tail risk, extreme value theory, expected shortfall, CVaR, conditional value at risk, risk estimation, financial risk modeling, portfolio risk, risk measurement, volatility forecasting, stress testing, market risk, quantile estimation, risk management, financial time series
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We propose a novel machine learning approach to value-at-risk estimation that combines extreme value theory with deep neural networks to better capture tail behavior in high-dimensional financial portfolios. Our method addresses limitations of traditional parametric and historical simulation approaches by adaptively learning nonlinear relationships between risk factors and extreme losses. We demonstrate superior out-of-sample performance on equity and fixed-income portfolios across multiple market regimes, with particular improvements in tail quantile forecasting during crisis periods. Backtesting results show our approach reduces both Type I and Type II errors compared to standard unconditional and conditional VaR models.

queries.persona: Senior fullstack engineer wanting to move into AI engineering.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We propose an end-to-end framework leveraging large language models to automate software engineering tasks including code review, bug detection, and test generation. Our approach fine-tunes transformer-based models on domain-specific software repositories and employs few-shot prompting strategies to generate contextually appropriate code reviews, identify potential defects, and synthesize unit tests. Experimental evaluation on open-source projects demonstrates that our LLM agent achieves 78% precision in bug detection and generates functionally correct test cases for 65% of target functions, significantly outperforming rule-based baselines while reducing manual effort in the software development lifecycle.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Retrieval-augmented generation systems rely critically on the quality of retrieved documents to ground language model outputs. We propose a framework for improving embedding-based retrieval by optimizing the alignment between query and document representations through contrastive learning objectives. We introduce novel evaluation metrics tailored to RAG performance and demonstrate that explicit relevance ranking supervision significantly improves downstream generation quality. Experiments on multiple retrieval benchmarks show our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining computational efficiency for production deployment.

queries.persona: Application security engineer, comfortable reading papers, wants a research-grade side project.
queries.query: machine learning for detecting vulnerabilities in source code

plan.interpretation: You're looking for papers on machine learning and deep learning techniques applied to automated vulnerability detection and security bug discovery in source code.
plan.anchorText: vulnerability detection, source code analysis, machine learning security, deep learning code, static analysis, program synthesis, adversarial examples, code representation learning, security defects, software vulnerabilities, neural networks code, ast parsing, bug detection, malware detection, code embeddings
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a deep learning framework for detecting security vulnerabilities in source code by learning rich semantic representations of program structure. Our approach combines graph neural networks over abstract syntax trees and data dependency graphs with transformer-based encoders to capture both local code patterns and long-range semantic dependencies indicative of common vulnerability classes. We evaluate on large-scale datasets including CWE-annotated real-world code and synthetic vulnerability benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art precision and recall on buffer overflows, SQL injection, use-after-free, and path traversal vulnerabilities. Our method generalizes across programming languages and outperforms traditional static analysis tools on novel vulnerability variants not seen during training.

queries.persona: Security analyst moving toward ML security.
queries.query: attacks on and defenses for machine learning models - adversarial examples, poisoning, model stealing

plan.interpretation: You're looking for research on adversarial attacks against ML models and corresponding defense mechanisms, including adversarial examples, data poisoning, model extraction, and robustness techniques.
plan.anchorText: adversarial examples, adversarial attacks, adversarial training, adversarial robustness, model robustness, poisoning attacks, data poisoning, model stealing, model extraction, backdoor attacks, adversarial perturbations, evasion attacks, defense mechanisms, robust neural networks, certified robustness, adversarial defense
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We study the vulnerability of deep neural networks to adversarial perturbations, where small imperceptible changes to inputs can cause misclassification. We propose a defense mechanism based on adversarial training that robustly increases model resilience against both white-box and black-box attacks. Additionally, we investigate data poisoning attacks where an adversary injects malicious samples during training, and model extraction attacks where attackers query the model to reverse-engineer its weights. We evaluate our approach against multiple attack vectors on image classification benchmarks and provide empirical evidence of improved robustness without significant accuracy loss.

queries.persona: Engineer in healthcare software (HIPAA world), wants transferable privacy-tech skills.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Federated learning enables distributed training without centralizing sensitive data, but privacy remains a critical concern. We present a practical framework combining federated averaging with per-sample differential privacy noise injection, providing formal privacy guarantees while maintaining model utility. Our approach employs privacy budget allocation strategies and adaptive noise scheduling to balance convergence speed and privacy loss. We demonstrate the method on healthcare datasets with implementation details and code, showing that modest privacy budgets achieve competitive accuracy on realistic tasks. We provide empirical analysis of privacy-utility tradeoffs and discuss deployment considerations for resource-constrained devices.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a practical anomaly detection framework for high-dimensional system telemetry combining unsupervised learning with statistical baselines. Our approach integrates time-series preprocessing, feature extraction from logs and metrics, and ensemble anomaly scoring to identify both point and collective anomalies in operational data. We evaluate on real production datasets spanning application metrics, system logs, and infrastructure telemetry, demonstrating detection rates above 85% with minimal false positives. The system handles concept drift and seasonal patterns common in operational environments, with latency suitable for real-time monitoring pipelines.

queries.persona: QA automation engineer who wants to stand out with research-informed tooling.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Automated software testing remains a challenge in continuous integration pipelines, particularly in detecting and resolving flaky tests that fail intermittently regardless of code changes. We propose a novel approach combining fuzzing-guided test generation with machine learning-based flakiness prediction to identify tests prone to non-deterministic failures. Our method analyzes execution traces, timing characteristics, and environmental dependencies across multiple test runs, achieving 87% precision in flaky test detection on real-world projects. We demonstrate the approach on three large open-source repositories, showing that our tooling reduces false failures by 73% and accelerates CI/CD cycles by 41%.

Experiments on small-scale datasets demonstrate competitive performance against heavier baselines while maintaining low computational overhead suitable for resource-constrained deployments. Our method achieves improved ranking quality and cold-start performance with minimal training data.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We propose a deep reinforcement learning framework for optimal trade execution that minimizes expected transaction costs while accounting for market impact and adverse selection. Our approach models the limit order book dynamics and uses a recurrent neural network to predict short-term price movements and liquidity conditions. We evaluate the algorithm on intraday equity data, demonstrating 15-20% reduction in implementation shortfall compared to classical VWAP and TWAP benchmarks. We further extend the framework to multi-asset execution with portfolio-level constraints and show that accounting for cross-asset correlations yields significant additional cost savings.

plan.anchorText: agentic systems, autonomous agents, large language models, financial document analysis, document processing, information extraction, natural language processing, multimodal document understanding, workflow automation, financial data extraction, retrieval-augmented generation, agent reasoning, tool use, document classification, named entity recognition, financial NLP
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present an agentic framework for automated financial document analysis that integrates large language models with document retrieval, entity extraction, and reasoning modules. The system processes diverse financial documents including SEC filings, earnings reports, and regulatory submissions, extracting key entities, relationships, and compliance-relevant information through multi-step reasoning with tool access. We demonstrate the approach on document classification, cross-document question answering, and risk signal extraction from unstructured text. Empirical results show significant improvements over baseline LLM approaches in accuracy and interpretability, with application to financial due diligence and compliance workflows.

queries.persona: ML engineer, 2 years, comfortable with PyTorch. Wants to get ahead of post-transformer architectures before they're mainstream.
queries.query: state space models like Mamba or S4 for long time series - is attention actually needed?

plan.interpretation: You're looking for research on state space models (Mamba, S4, and related architectures) as alternatives to attention-based transformers for sequence modeling, with focus on long-range dependencies and time series applications.
plan.anchorText: state space models, Mamba, S4, selective state space, long sequence modeling, attention alternatives, sequence-to-sequence learning, long-range dependencies, time series forecasting, recurrent neural networks, linear complexity sequences, efficient transformers, structured state spaces, autoregressive modeling, temporal pattern recognition
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We propose a systematic study of state space model architectures for long-range sequence modeling, comparing linear recurrent and semi-structured state space methods against transformer attention. Our key contribution is a unified framework analyzing how selective gating and parameterized state transitions enable effective context compression without quadratic attention complexity. We evaluate on long-context language modeling, time series forecasting, and synthetic tasks requiring 4K-16K token dependencies. Results demonstrate that properly designed SSM variants match or exceed transformer performance while maintaining linear or subquadratic complexity. We further provide ablations isolating the role of attention mechanisms, showing that structured state transitions and gating often subsume attention benefits. Code and benchmarks released for reproducibility.

queries.persona: Backend developer self-studying LLM fine-tuning on a single 24GB GPU at home.
queries.query: direct preference optimization vs RLHF for aligning small open models, something I can run myself

plan.interpretation: You want papers comparing direct preference optimization (DPO) with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for aligning small language models, with focus on methods feasible to implement and run on consumer hardware.
plan.anchorText: direct preference optimization, DPO, RLHF, reinforcement learning human feedback, preference learning, language model alignment, model alignment training, fine-tuning methods, small language models, open-source models, reward modeling, policy optimization, inference efficiency, parameter-efficient fine-tuning
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Aligning large language models with human preferences typically requires computationally expensive reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with separate reward models. We propose and evaluate direct preference optimization (DPO), a simpler approach that extracts preference information directly from preference pairs without explicit reward modeling or reinforcement learning. We compare DPO against standard RLHF on open-source models up to 7B parameters, demonstrating comparable or superior alignment quality with significantly reduced training compute and memory requirements. Our results show DPO achieves comparable downstream task performance while being tractable on consumer-grade hardware, making preference-based alignment accessible for practitioners fine-tuning smaller open models.

queries.persona: Infrastructure engineer at a startup that serves LLM endpoints; latency is the pain point.
queries.query: speculative decoding or other LLM inference speedups I could benchmark on one GPU

plan.interpretation: You want to find papers on techniques to accelerate LLM inference—such as speculative decoding, distillation, quantization, or other methods—that you could benchmark and potentially implement on a single GPU.
plan.anchorText: speculative decoding, LLM inference acceleration, language model speedup, token generation latency, distillation, quantization, pruning, batching optimization, autoregressive sampling, early exit, knowledge distillation, model compression, inference efficiency, throughput optimization, GPU memory optimization
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a practical framework for accelerating large language model inference through speculative decoding, a technique that generates multiple candidate tokens in parallel and verifies them against the model in a single forward pass. Our method reduces per-token latency by leveraging lightweight drafting models to predict probable continuations, then validates predictions using cached key-value tensors to amortize compute cost. We demonstrate speedups of 1.5–2.5× on various model sizes (7B–70B parameters) running on single-GPU hardware, with analysis of memory bandwidth trade-offs and draft model scaling. Experiments on standard benchmarks show consistent improvements across different sampling strategies and show negligible impact on output quality.

queries.persona: Platform engineer optimizing an internal LLM gateway with long-context workloads.
queries.query: KV cache compression and quantization techniques for serving long context models

plan.interpretation: You're looking for papers on key-value cache compression and quantization methods that enable efficient serving of long-context language models in production systems.
plan.anchorText: KV cache compression, quantization, long-context language models, token attention, key-value compression techniques, serving inference, memory-efficient transformers, attention mechanism optimization, model quantization, inference latency reduction, long sequence processing, attention cache optimization
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: The key-value cache in transformer-based language models becomes a significant memory and latency bottleneck during long-context inference. We propose a multi-stage KV cache compression pipeline combining selective token pruning, post-training quantization to 4-bit precision, and learned low-rank decomposition of cache matrices. Our method maintains generation quality on long-context benchmarks while reducing cache memory by 6.5× and improving throughput by 3.2× on commodity GPUs. We demonstrate the approach on models up to 70B parameters and show compatibility with existing speculative decoding optimizations, achieving practical speedups on real serving workloads.

queries.persona: Former product manager turned junior developer; explains AI features to non-technical stakeholders.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Large language models frequently generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect statements with high confidence. We present a framework for detecting hallucinated outputs by combining uncertainty quantification from transformer hidden states with external knowledge verification. We evaluate our approach on multiple QA and dialogue benchmarks, achieving 78% hallucination detection accuracy while maintaining 92% recall on factual outputs. We additionally propose a training-time intervention using contrastive learning to reduce model confidence on out-of-distribution and unseen facts. Human evaluation confirms our method identifies problematic outputs before deployment in conversational agents.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Deep neural networks achieve state-of-the-art accuracy but remain computationally expensive for deployment on mobile and edge devices. We propose a unified compression framework combining structured pruning, post-training quantization, and knowledge distillation to reduce model size and inference latency while maintaining task performance. Experiments on ImageNet and mobile vision tasks demonstrate 8-10× compression and 4-6× speedup with <2% accuracy drop compared to full-precision baselines. The compressed models run efficiently on ARM processors without specialized hardware, enabling real-time inference on smartphones. Code and pre-compressed models are released for practitioners.

queries.persona: High-school CS teacher who builds tools on the side; students keep submitting suspicious essays.
queries.query: how do people tell if a piece of text was written by an AI

plan.interpretation: You're looking for papers on detecting AI-generated text and distinguishing it from human writing.
plan.anchorText: AI text detection, language model detection, GPT detection, machine-generated text identification, natural language authenticity verification, neural text generation detection, deepfake text, watermarking language models, statistical signatures of neural language models, stylometry for detection, transformer output classification, text classification for synthetic detection
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Detecting machine-generated text has become increasingly critical as large language models produce increasingly human-like outputs. We present a comprehensive analysis of linguistic signatures that distinguish text written by modern language models from human-authored content, including syntactic patterns, semantic coherence markers, and statistical distributional properties. We evaluate detection methods across multiple architectures (GPT-2, GPT-3, BERT-based generators) and datasets, achieving 87% F1-score on out-of-distribution test sets. Our findings reveal that detectors relying on single statistical features are vulnerable to adversarial paraphrasing, while ensemble approaches combining syntax, perplexity, and token-level confidence scores show robust generalization. We release an open-source detector and discuss implications for academic integrity and content authenticity verification.

queries.persona: Data scientist at a 20-person company; labeling budget is basically zero.

plan.interpretation: Finding techniques and methods for training effective machine learning models with minimal labeled data, including semi-supervised, self-supervised, and low-data learning approaches.
plan.anchorText: semi-supervised learning, self-supervised learning, few-shot learning, transfer learning, weak supervision, data augmentation, active learning, label-efficient training, pseudo-labeling, contrastive learning, unsupervised pretraining, domain adaptation, zero-shot learning, unlabeled data utilization, meta-learning
plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We address the challenge of training high-performance machine learning models when labeled data is severely limited. We propose a practical framework combining semi-supervised learning with self-supervised pretraining and active learning strategies to maximize model performance given a fixed annotation budget. Our method leverages unlabeled data through consistency regularization and pseudo-labeling while strategically selecting the most informative samples for manual labeling. Experiments on image and text classification tasks demonstrate that our approach achieves 85-92% of fully-supervised performance while requiring only 5-10% of the labeled data, making it suitable for resource-constrained production settings.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We investigate prompt injection and jailbreak vulnerabilities in large language models, where carefully crafted inputs circumvent intended constraints to elicit harmful or unintended behaviors. We present a taxonomy of attack techniques spanning direct instruction override, context confusion, and constraint relaxation. We evaluate these attacks across multiple LLM architectures and propose practical defense mechanisms including input sanitization, prompt anchoring, and constraint verification. Our evaluation demonstrates that combining syntactic and semantic defenses reduces attack success rates by up to 85% while maintaining utility. We provide open-source tooling for security auditing and defense validation.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Accurate cardinality estimation is critical for query optimization, yet traditional statistical models struggle with complex predicates and correlated attributes. We propose a machine learning-based cardinality estimator that leverages deep neural networks to learn complex patterns in table statistics and query predicates, trained end-to-end on a workload of historical queries. Experiments on benchmark TPC-H and real OLTP workloads show 2-3× improvements in query plan quality compared to PostgreSQL's default statistics-based estimator, with learned models that adapt dynamically to data distribution shifts. Our approach integrates seamlessly with existing query optimizers through a drop-in replacement for the selectivity computation layer, enabling immediate deployment in production databases.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a framework for training machine learning models on sensitive financial transaction data while satisfying differential privacy guarantees required by regulatory compliance. The method combines federated learning with local differential privacy mechanisms to enable secure aggregation of transaction features across distributed payment systems without centralizing raw data. We demonstrate the approach on transaction classification and anomaly detection tasks, showing that privacy budgets compatible with GDPR Article 32 and PCI-DSS requirements can be achieved with acceptable utility-privacy tradeoffs. Our implementation uses secure multi-party computation primitives to protect intermediate model updates, and we validate compliance through formal privacy audits on real payment network data.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a graph neural network framework for detecting organized fraud rings and money laundering networks in financial transaction data. Our approach represents accounts as nodes and transactions as directed edges with attributes, applying graph convolutional layers to learn node embeddings that capture network structure and temporal patterns. We introduce a novel loss function combining graph-level anomaly scoring with local subgraph pattern detection to identify coordinated illicit behavior. Evaluated on synthetic and real financial networks, our method achieves 87% precision in detecting fraud rings while maintaining low false positive rates compared to transaction-level baselines, and demonstrates interpretability through subgraph visualization of detected criminal networks.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: We present a practical framework for pretraining and fine-tuning language models on resource-constrained consumer devices. We demonstrate generalization across multiple hardware platforms and manipulation primitives, providing practical guidance for practitioners deploying learning-based control on resource-constrained robotic systems.

plan.hypotheticalAbstract: Medical image reconstruction from undersampled or sparse data remains a computational bottleneck in clinical practice. We propose a deep learning architecture that learns to invert the forward imaging model, reconstructing high-fidelity CT and MRI volumes from reduced-dose acquisitions and undersampled k-space data respectively. Our method combines unrolled optimization steps with convolutional feature extraction to enforce both data consistency and learned image priors. On benchmark CT and multi-contrast MRI datasets, our approach outperforms classical iterative methods in reconstruction speed and quality, while enabling end-to-end segmentation of anatomical structures. We validate on clinical data and demonstrate clinically viable inference times.
